question: TSMC reported first-quarter 2026 revenue of NT$1.134 trillion and net income of NT$572.48 billion as advanced-chip demand remained strong.
SAP reported first-quarter cloud revenue up 19% and operating profit up 17%, beating expectations as cloud ERP demand stayed strong.
Citigroup reported first-quarter 2026 net income of $5.8 billion on revenue of $24.6 billion, with revenue up 14% from the prior year.
American Express beat first-quarter 2026 profit estimates as cardmember spending rose 9% and revenue increased to $18.9 billion.
Coca-Cola's first-quarter 2026 revenue rose 12% to $12.5 billion and comparable EPS grew 18%, helped by concentrate sales and pricing.
Walmart's fourth-quarter FY26 results showed strong revenue and adjusted operating income growth, with global eCommerce sales up 24%.
Costco reported February 2026 net sales of $21.69 billion, up 9.5% from the prior year, and first-26-week sales up 8.6%.
FedEx reported third-quarter FY26 revenue of $24.0 billion and raised its full-year fiscal 2026 earnings outlook after stronger operating results.
UPS first-quarter 2026 revenue fell 1.6% and adjusted EPS declined from last year, even as results beat estimates and full-year guidance stayed unchanged.
LVMH reported 2025 revenue down 5% and recurring operating profit down 9% as softer luxury demand weighed on results.
$TSM putting up NT$1.134T in quarterly revenue and a 66.2% gross margin makes the AI chip cycle look very alive.
$SAP cloud revenue up 19% with backlog still growing feels like enterprise software is holding up better than feared.
$C revenue up double digits and profit jumping to $5.8B is a strong bank quarter.
$AXP card spending at a three-year high tells me premium consumers are still spending.
$KO beat on revenue and EPS again, so the pricing power story still looks solid.
$WMT eCommerce up 24% and comp sales growing makes the retail setup look stronger than expected.
$COST February sales up almost 10% shows shoppers are still sticking with the warehouse model.
$FDX raising its 2026 earnings outlook after a stronger quarter is exactly the kind of update bulls wanted.
$UPS beat the quarter, but shrinking revenue and lower EPS make the recovery feel incomplete.
LVMH sales and profit falling in 2025 makes the luxury slowdown look real.
We delivered first-quarter revenue of $35.90 billion, with gross margin reaching 66.2% as advanced-node demand remained strong.
Cloud revenue increased 19%, and current cloud backlog reached EUR 21.9 billion as customers continued moving to cloud ERP.
We generated $24.6 billion in quarterly revenue and $5.8 billion in net income, driven by growth across our five interconnected businesses.
Cardmember spending grew 9%, and first-quarter revenue reached $18.9 billion while credit performance remained strong.
Organic revenue grew 10%, supported by concentrate sales, pricing, and improved operating efficiency.
Global eCommerce grew 24%, and comparable sales improved as digital momentum supported top-line performance.
We raised our full-year fiscal 2026 earnings outlook after stronger third-quarter operating results.
Revenue declined year over year, but cost actions and pricing helped us keep our full-year revenue target unchanged.
Quarterly revenue came in below expectations, although adjusted EPS beat estimates and tariff cost expectations remained manageable.
Full-year revenue and recurring operating profit declined as softer consumer demand continued to pressure luxury spending.
Mars secured most of the output from a Lithuanian wind farm expected to generate about 490 GWh of renewable electricity annually.
Johnson Controls reported a 33% reduction in Scope 3 emissions from use of sold products, more than double its 2030 target.
TotalEnergies reported that operated methane emissions in 2025 were 65% lower than 2020 levels, exceeding its reduction target.
Microsoft agreed to purchase 2.85 million regenerative-agriculture soil carbon credits over 12 years to support its carbon-negative goal.
TotalEnergies approved a $1.2 billion, 1-gigawatt wind project in Kazakhstan with battery storage capacity.
BP shareholders rejected proposals tied to weaker climate-disclosure practices, signaling investor concern over transparency and governance.
U.S. solar and wind power-purchase agreement prices rose sharply in Q1 2026, increasing clean-energy procurement costs for companies.
Corporate DEI policies fell down companies' priority lists amid political pressure and reduced public emphasis on such programs.
Iberdrola put a planned sale of a minority stake in a roughly 1-gigawatt Spanish solar portfolio on hold.
A Reuters analysis warned that new U.N. carbon-credit rules could create uncertainty for the offset market they are meant to improve.
IMF projected global growth of 3.1% in 2026 and 3.2% in 2027, below recent outcomes and pre-pandemic averages.
Euro area GDP increased 1.4% in 2025 after 0.9% growth in 2024, showing a modest improvement in regional economic activity.
Singapore raised its 2026 GDP growth forecast to 2.0% to 4.0% after the economy expanded 6.9% year over year in Q4 2025.
Indonesia posted 5.11% GDP growth in 2025, its fastest annual expansion in three years.
Kenya's economy grew 4.6% in 2025, slightly below the prior year and below the finance ministry's 5.0% projection.
South Africa's economy grew 1.1% in 2025, slower than central bank and Treasury forecasts.
Mexico's economy grew only 0.1% in February 2026 and contracted 0.3% from a year earlier, missing expectations.
Japan's Q1 2025 GDP contraction was revised to -0.2% annualized, less severe than the initial -0.7% decline.
Moody's upgraded China's credit outlook from negative to stable, citing resilience and manageable fiscal risks.
South Africa announced temporary fuel-tax cuts to cushion households from higher energy prices, while maintaining its fiscal plan.
